reasoning: The forecast is based on the NASA GISTEMP index anomalies (base period 1951-1980) for October 2025. Historically, only the peak El Niño years of 2023 and 2024 have ever exceeded 1.10°C in October (e.g. 2015 reached 1.07°C). With ENSO patterns showing El Niño had faded and NOAA forecasting a 71% chance of a developing La Niña by late 2025, temperatures are in a cooling phase relative to the 2023-2024 peak. Search results reveal July 2025 anomaly was reported as 1.25°C above pre-industrial (1850-1900) levels, which corresponds to roughly 0.97°C in the GISTEMP baseline. Annual forecasts predict an average warming of ~1.4°C (relative to 1850-1900) for 2025, suggesting that typical late-year months must be well below the average to counteract the record-breaking warmth of early 2025. During developing La Niña years, anomalies usually remain flat or decrease into autumn, making a bounce back up to 1.10°C (which requires a pre-industrial equivalent of ~1.38°C) very unlikely. Small uncertainties remain regarding month-to-month volatility (weather noise) or underlying permanent shifts from reduced aerosol cooling, but the evidence strongly suggests October 2025 will be below the 1.10°C threshold.
